author: Augustine of Hippo

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

ccel.title: NPNF1-07. St. Augustine: Homilies on the Gospel of John; Homilies on the First Epistle of John; Soliloquies

source.title: NPNF1-07. St. Augustine: Homilies on the Gospel of John; Homilies on the First Epistle of John; Soliloquies

title: Chapter XVI. 23–28.

source.title: NPNF1-07. St. Augustine: Homilies on the Gospel of John; Homilies on the First Epistle of John; Soliloquies

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: We have now to consider these words of the Lord, “Verily, verily, I say unto you, If ye shall ask anything of the Father in my name, He will give it you.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: It has already been said in the earlier portions of this discourse of our Lord’s, on account of those who ask some things of the Father in Christ’s name and receive them not, that there is nothing asked of the Father in the Saviour’s name that is asked in contrariety to the method of salvation.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For it is not the sound of the letters and syllables, but what the sound itself imports, and what is rightly and truly to be understood by that sound, that He is to be regarded as declaring, when He says, “in my name.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Hence, he who has such ideas of Christ as ought not to be entertained of the only Son of God, asketh not in His name, even though he may not abstain from the mention of Christ in so many letters and syllables; since it is only in His name he asketh, of whom he is thinking when he asketh.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But he who has such ideas of Him as ought to be entertained, asketh in His name, and receiveth what he asketh, if he asketh nothing that is contrary to his own everlasting salvation.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And he receiveth it when he ought to receive it.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For some things are not refused, but are delayed till they can be given at a suitable time.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: In this way, surely, we are to understand His words, “He will give you,” so that thereby we may know that those benefits are signified which are properly applicable to those who ask.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For all the saints are heard effectively in their own behalf, but are not so heard in behalf of all besides, whether friends or enemies, or any others: for it is not said in a general kind of way, “He will give;” but, “He will give you.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “Hitherto,” He says, “ye have not asked anything in my name.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Ask, and ye shall receive, that your joy may be full.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: This that He calls a full joy is certainly no carnal joy, but a spiritual one; and when it shall be so great as to be no longer capable of any additions to it, it will then doubtless be full.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Whatever, then, is asked as belonging to the attainment of this joy, is to be asked in the name of Christ, if we understand the grace of God, and if we are truly in quest of a blessed life.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But if aught different from this is asked, there is nothing asked: not that the thing itself is nothing at all, but that in comparison with what is so great, anything else that is coveted is virtually nothing.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For, of course, the man is not actually nothing, of whom the apostle says, “He who thinketh himself to be something, when he is nothing.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But surely in comparison with the spiritual man, who knows that by the grace of God he is what he is, he who makes vain assumptions is nothing.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: In this way, then, may the words also be rightly understood, “Verily, verily, I say unto you, if ye shall ask anything of the Father in my name, He will give [it] you;” that by the words, “if anything,” should not be understood anything whatever, but anything that is not really nothing in connection with the life of blessedness.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And what follows, “Hitherto ye have not asked anything in my name,” may be understood in two ways: either, that ye have not asked in my name, because a name that ye have not known as it is yet to be known; or, ye have not asked anything, since in comparison with that which ye ought to have asked, what ye have asked is to be accounted as nothing.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: In order, then, that, they may ask in His name, not that which is nothing, but a full joy (since anything different from this that they ask is virtually nothing), He addresses to them the exhortation, “Ask, and ye shall receive, that your joy may be full;” that is, ask this in my name, that your joy may be full, and ye shall receive.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For His saints, who persevere in asking such a good thing as this, will in no wise be defrauded by the mercy of God.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “These things,” said He, “have I spoken to you in proverbs: but the hour cometh, when I shall no more speak unto you in proverbs, but I shall show you plainly of my Father.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: I might be disposed to say that this hour, whereof He speaketh, must be understood as that future period when we shall see openly, as the blessed Paul says, “face to face;” that what He says, “These things have I spoken to you in proverbs,” is one with what has been said by the same apostle, “Now we see through a glass, in a riddle:” and “I will show you,” because the Father shall be seen through the instrumentality of the Son, is akin to what He says elsewhere, “Neither knoweth any man the Father, save the Son, and [he] to whom the Son shall be pleased to reveal Him.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But such a sense seems to be interfered with by that which follows: “At that day ye shall ask in my name.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For in that future world, when we have reached the kingdom where we shall be like Him, for we shall see Him as He is, what shall we then have to ask, when our desire shall be satisfied with good things?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: As it is also said in another psalm: “I shall be satisfied when Thy glory shall be revealed.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For petition has to do with some kind of want, which can have no place there where such abundance shall reign.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: It remains, therefore, for us, so far as my capacity to apprehend it goes, to understand Jesus as having promised that He would cause His disciples, from being carnal and natural, to become spiritual, although not yet such as we shall be, when a spiritual body shall also be ours; but such as was he who said, “We speak wisdom among them that are perfect;” and, “I could not speak unto you as unto spiritual, but as unto carnal;” and, “We have received, not the spirit of the world, but the Spirit who is of God; that we might know the things that are freely given to us of God.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Which things also we speak, not in the words which man’s wisdom teacheth, but which the Spirit teacheth; comparing spiritual things with spiritual.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But the natural man perceiveth not the things of the Spirit of God.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And thus the natural man, perceiving not the things of the Spirit of God, hears in such a way whatever is told him of the nature of God, that he can conceive of nothing else but some bodily form, however spacious or immense, however lustrous and magnificent, yet still a body: and therefore he holds as proverbs all that is said of the incorporeal and immutable substance of wisdom; not that he accounts them as proverbs, but that his thoughts follow the same direction as those who habitually listen to proverbs without understanding them.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But when the spiritual man begins to discern all things, and he himself is discerned by no man, he perceives, even though in this life it still be through a glass and in part, not by any bodily sense, and not by any imaginative conception which catches at or devises the likenesses of all sorts of bodies, but by the clearest understanding of the mind, that God is not material, but spiritual: in such a way does the Son show us openly of the Father, that He, who thus shows, is also Himself seen to be of the same substance.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And then it is that those who ask, ask in His name; for in the sound of that name they understand nothing else than what the reality is that is called by that name, and harbor not, in vanity or infirmity of mind, the fiction of the Father being in one place, and the Son in another, standing before the Father and making request in our behalf, with the material substances of both occupying each its own place, and the Word pleading verbally for us with Him whose Word He is, while a definite space interposes between the mouth of the speaker and the ears of the hearer; and other such absurdities which those who are natural, and at the same time carnal, fabricate for themselves in their hearts.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For any such thing, suggested by the experience of bodily habits, as occurs to spiritual men when thinking of God, they deny and reject, and drive away, like troublesome insects, from the eyes of their mind; and resign themselves to the purity of that light by whose testimony and judgment they prove these bodily images that thrust themselves on their inward vision to be altogether false.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: These are able to a certain extent to think of our Lord Jesus Christ, in respect of His manhood, as addressing the Father on our behalf; but in respect to His Godhead, as hearing [and answering] us along with the Father.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And this I am of opinion that He indicated, when He said, “And I say not that I will pray the Father for you.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But the intuitive perception of this, how it is that the Son asketh not the Father, but that Father and Son alike listen to those who ask, is a height that can be reached only by the spiritual eye of the mind.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “For the Father Himself,” He says, “loveth you, because ye have loved me.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Is it the case, then, that He loveth, because we love; or rather, that we love, because He loveth?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Let this same evangelist give us the answer out of his own epistle: “We love Him,” he says, “because He first loved us.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: This, then, was the efficient cause of our loving, that we were loved.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And certainly to love God is the gift of God.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: He it was that gave the grace to love Him, who loved while still unloved.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Even when displeasing Him we were loved, that there might be that in us whereby we should become pleasing in His sight.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For we could not love the Son unless we loved the Father also.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: The Father loveth us, because we love the Son; seeing it is of the Father and Son we have received [the power] to love both the Father and the Son: for love is shed abroad in our hearts by the Spirit of both, by which Spirit we love both the Father and the Son, and whom we love along with the Father and the Son.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: God, therefore, it was that wrought this religious love of ours whereby we worship God; and He saw that it is good, and on that account He Himself loved that which He had made.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But He would not have wrought in us something He could love, were it not that He loved ourselves before He wrought it.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “And ye have believed,” He adds, “that I came out from God.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: I came forth from the Father, and am come into the world: again I leave the world, and go to the Father.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Clearly we have believed.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For surely it ought not to be accounted a thing incredible because of this, that in coming to the world He came forth in such a sense from the Father that He did not leave the Father behind; and that, on leaving the world, He goes to the Father in such a sense that He does not actually forsake the world.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For He came forth from the Father because He is of the Father; and He came into the world, in showing to the world His bodily form, which He had received of the Virgin.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: He left the world by a bodily withdrawal, He proceeded to the Father by His ascension as man, but He forsook not the world in the ruling activity of His presence.
